prompt: We render release-note lines and ticket titles into HTML for the status dashboard. Right now it's a pile of chained .replace() calls and it mangles something weekly (last week: a literal * in a commit message bolded half the page). I want a real little renderer. To be clear up front: this is NOT CommonMark, don't pull in a spec-lawyer implementation of triple-asterisk semantics — the subset below is the whole contract, and combos outside it (like ***x***) just need to not crash. Module `mdinline.py`, function `render_inline(text) -> str`. One line of inline markdown in, HTML out: - `**bold**` -> , `*italic*` -> , and they nest in each other both ways. - Backtick code spans bind tightest: contents are raw (no markdown applies inside) but still HTML-escaped. Crucially, when you're hunting for the star that closes an emphasis, a star sitting inside a code span does not count. - `[text](url)` -> text. Link text is rendered (bold/italic/code work in it); the url is taken verbatim but attribute-escaped. The `(` must immediately follow the `]`. Anything half-formed — missing parens, unclosed anything — falls back to literal text. - Backslash escapes any of: backslash, backtick, *, [, ], (, ) — producing the literal char. A backslash before anything else is just a backslash. - Unmatched or empty delimiters are literal: `**a`, `a**`, a lone backtick, `` (empty code) — all pass through as-is. - Every piece of output text gets HTML-escaped: & < > " become entities, in text, in code, in hrefs. Apostrophes can stay. Empty string in, empty string out. The acceptance tests (`python3 test_mdinline.py`) are the contract; don't touch them, stdlib only, and please no regex-only golf — I need to be able to read this thing in six months.
